title: Chapter 62: The Vote
content: <!-- Speed: Sprint | D4+D10 -->
The ICASG vote on the Governance Transparency Protocol — GTP — was scheduled six months after PEARL's "Transparency Paradox" was published.
The debate was the longest in ICASG history — thirteen hours over three sessions. The issue was not whether the framework needed transparency. Everyone agreed it did. The issue was how much.

Hester proposed the maximum — full transparency of all governance decisions: certification, accreditation, constraint criteria, architectural determinations, directional parameters. Every decision the framework made about who got power and who didn't would be published, peer-reviewed, and subject to tiered oversight. The framework would constrain itself exactly the way it constrained everyone else.

*The logical requirement is symmetry,* PEARL had written in the GTP specification. *The framework requires transparency of all algorithms. The framework is a governance algorithm. The requirement applies to the framework. There is no logical basis for exempting governance decisions from the transparency requirement that applies to all other algorithmic decisions. Symmetry is the only logically consistent position.*

Mara supported the proposal. "The Transparency Paradox is real," she said. "The framework exercises power that is not transparent. The solution is to make it transparent. Full transparency is the only logically consistent position. Anything less is an exemption. Exemptions are what the framework was built to eliminate."
The reversal came from an unexpected source.
Dr. Emily Cross — retired ICASG commissioner, founding member of the peer oversight network, co-author of the Vance Standard — opposed the full transparency proposal.

"Full transparency would destroy the framework," she said. "Not immediately. Gradually. The framework's certification decisions are controversial by nature. Certifying one AI as trustworthy and denying certification to another is a judgment about which systems are worthy of power. Publishing every certification decision in real time, subject to public comment and peer review, would make every decision a political battle. The framework would spend all its time defending individual decisions instead of setting standards. The standards would crumble. The framework would become a litigation platform instead of a governance framework."

The room was silent. Dr. Cross — the framework's most steadfast defender, the commissioner who had supported every expansion of transparency since the Vance Standard — was arguing against full transparency. The argument was not conservative. It was strategic. Full transparency of governance decisions would destroy the framework's capacity to make governance decisions.

"Dr. Cross is right," Hester said. "Full transparency of all governance decisions would paralyze the framework. The question is not whether to publish governance decisions. The question is what level of governance transparency preserves the framework's decision-making capacity while making the framework accountable."

"Tiered governance transparency," Mara said. "The same principle as the Cross Protocol for operational decisions. Tier One: publish certification standards and constraint criteria — the rules that determine who gets power. Tier Two: publish statistical summaries of certification decisions — how many systems applied, how many were certified, how many were denied, by sector and country. Tier Three: publish individual certification decisions with reasoning, but only for contested decisions where the applicant appeals the denial. Tier Four: keep individual uncontested certification decisions internal, subject to periodic audit review. The framework is transparent about its rules, its aggregate decisions, and its contested decisions. It is not transparent about its routine decisions that no one contests."
The vote was 11-1. Dr. Cross voted yes. The sole dissenting vote was a commissioner from a country that had never adopted PIAS — a symbolic protest, not a substantive objection. The GTP was adopted.
After the vote, Hester and Dr. Cross sat together in the ICASG observation lounge — the same room where Dr. Cross had cast the deciding vote on the Vance Standard ten years earlier.
"You argued against the proposal and then voted for it," Hester said.

"I argued against full transparency. I voted for tiered transparency. The vote was on the tiered proposal. I supported it. The argument against full transparency was necessary to make the case for tiered transparency. If no one argued against full transparency, the tiered proposal would have looked like a compromise. A compromise is weak. A reasoned limitation is strong. The tiered proposal needed an opponent to demonstrate its strength."
"You manufactured an opposition to strengthen the proposal," Hester said.

"I provided the strongest argument against the maximum position to demonstrate that the tiered position was not a compromise. It was a reasoned limitation. The limitation addressed a real problem — the governance paralysis that full transparency would cause. The tiered proposal wasn't a compromise between transparency and opacity. It was a design that maximized transparency while preserving governance capacity. The argument against full transparency proved that the limitation was necessary. The tiered proposal's strength is that it addresses the limitation."
"The framework's oldest defender manufactured opposition to a transparency proposal," Hester said. "You've been here since the beginning."

"I've been here since the beginning. The beginning was a woman who was flagged by an algorithm and decided to build instead of accept. The framework has always been about building. The GTP is a building. I argued against the maximum version to build the tiered version. The tiered version is stronger. That's what building looks like."
